original_abstract: Replacing energy-intensive cryogenic distillation with highly efficient adsorptive separation remains a primary goal in chemical engineering, yet achieving both high selectivity and high capacity through molecular sieving-type adsorbents is a persistent challenge. Here, we report the development of **UTSA-400**, an oxyfluoride-based ultramicroporous metal-organic framework with the formula [Ni(WO₂F₄)(pyz)₂] (pyz = pyrazine). This material features one-dimensional pore channels engineered to accommodate propylene molecules with optimal binding affinity while strictly excluding propane. Within the framework, exposed oxide/fluoride pairs serve as potent functional sites that strengthen host-guest interactions, resulting in significantly enhanced propylene uptake. In contrast, propane molecules are entirely rejected due to the precisely regulated dynamics of the host framework. This robust binding environment allows propylene to reach near-saturation levels within the pore confinement under ambient conditions, maximizing pore space utilization and achieving superior packing density. The nature of these enhanced interactions was elucidated through a combination of in situ infrared spectroscopy and dispersion-corrected density functional theory (DFT) calculations. Breakthrough experiments confirm that **UTSA-400** can directly produce polymer-grade (>99.5%) propylene with remarkable dynamic productivity. This work demonstrates how atomic-precision pore engineering can optimize guest binding to overcome the traditional trade-offs in adsorptive gas separation.
humanized_abstract: Although highly efficient adsorptive separation of gas mixtures of increasing complexity has become a focal point in chemical engineering, achieving, via molecular sieving-type adsorbents, high selectivity and high capacity remains a persistent challenge. We present oxyfluoride metal-organic framework UTSA-400, [Ni(WO₂F₄)(pyz)₂] (pyz = pyrazine), composed of a stable 3D structure containing open 1D micropores that can selectively bind propylene molecules at the optimal strength while rejecting propane. The oxide/fluoride pairs on the surface of the 3D structure significantly increase the host-guest interactions that enhance the propylene uptake. The adsorbed propane is rejected because the host framework adjusts its geometry so that propane cannot be accommodated inside the pores. In the pores of UTSA-400, propylene is adsorbed at nearly saturated levels under ambient conditions while reaching high packing density. Detailed insights into the nature of the strong binding of propylene in the 1D micropores were gained by combining in situ IR spectroscopy measurements and dispersion corrected density functional theory (DFT) studies. Breakthrough experiments demonstrate that polymer grade (>99.5%) propylene can be produced directly by UTSA-400 while maintaining high dynamic productivity.

We present oxyfluoride metal-organic framework UTSA-400, [Ni(WO₂F₄)(pyz)₂] (pyz = pyrazine), composed of a stable 3D structure containing open 1D micropores that can selectively bind propylene molecules at the optimal strength while rejecting propane. The oxide/fluoride pairs on the surface of the 3D structure significantly increase the host-guest interactions that enhance the propylene uptake. The adsorbed propane is rejected because the host framework adjusts its geometry so that propane cannot be accommodated inside the pores. In the pores of UTSA-400, propylene is adsorbed at nearly saturated levels under ambient conditions while reaching high packing density. Detailed insights into the nature of the strong binding of propylene in the 1D micropores were gained by combining in situ IR spectroscopy measurements and dispersion corrected density functional theory (DFT) studies. Breakthrough experiments demonstrate that polymer grade (>99.5%) propylene can be produced directly by UTSA-400 while maintaining high dynamic productivity.
